preamble: The Voice Loss of the initial dental stop [d] to [t] is a major driver of Tonogenesis through Register Split. Voiced initials like [d] traditionally lowered the Pitch Contour (leading to 'Yáng' or low-register tones), while their voiceless counterparts [t] produced higher pitch ('Yīn' or high-register tones).
phoneticEffects: Voice Loss, Devoicing, Register Split, Tonogenesis

examples.note: Initial voicing loss in Mon-Khmer expanded the Vietnamese tonal inventory by splitting existing tones into high and low register pairs.
Part of the systemic devoicing of initial obstruents which doubled the tonal categories.
Early Middle Chinese [d] (Quánzhuó) devoiced in later stages, causing a split in the four classical tones.
Proto-Tai initial *d triggered the low-register tones in the subsequent Thai tonal split.
Led to the split of the historic tones into Low and High registers based on initial voicing.

examples.to: t- (aspirated or unaspirated based on tone)
examples.note: The low register of modern Cantonese tones reflects original voicing in the initial.

sources: Haudricourt, A. G. (1954). De l'origine des tons en viêtnamien.
Pittayaporn, P. (2009). The Phonology of Proto-Tai.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
